beats.text: You learned to choose scales. You checked if the biggest number fits. You checked the last digits. Now let's connect what you discovered.

condition.selected: Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?

incorrects.description: Student selected 'Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?', which is the second check, not the first

steps.text: You need to check if the biggest number fits first. If the number is too big, the scale won't work at all. Which question checks that?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. First, you check if the biggest number fits. That tells you which scales are even possible. A scale that's too small won't work, no matter what. Then, after you know it fits, you check if the numbers end in 0 or 5. That tells you which scale makes the counting easiest. The answer is: Does the biggest number fit? That's always first.
Graph A shows every single number, but count the tick marks. Too many lines make it harder to read quickly. When data is bigger, a larger scale is cleaner.
Graph B has fewer lines than A, but still a lot to count. Look at the values: 20, 40, 60. They're all multiples of 10. Which scale matches that pattern best?
Graph C is cleaner than A and B. But look at Graph D. When all your numbers end in 0, the scale of 10 has the fewest lines and is easiest to read.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Graph D highlights. Tick marks at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 pulse to show clean spacing.

steps.text: Here's how to think about this. All the values are 20, 40, and 60. Those are multiples of 10. Graph D uses a scale of 10, so every bar lands exactly on a line. Fewer tick marks means less clutter. That makes Graph D the easiest to read. When your data fits a bigger scale, use it.

incorrects.description: Student selected Table A, Daily Steps with values 4500, 3200, 5100, 2800

steps.text: Look at the numbers in Table A. They all end in hundreds: 4,500, 3,200, 5,100, 2,800. You could use Scale of 5 or even larger scales. Which table has numbers that don't end in 0 or 5?

incorrects.description: Student selected Table C, Books Read with values 25, 30, 35, 40

steps.text: Table C has 25, 30, 35, 40. Every number ends in 0 or 5. You could use Scale of 5 or Scale of 10 for those. Which table has numbers that aren't multiples of 5?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Table B highlights and its values pulse: 7, 12, 19, 23.

steps.text: Here's how to think about it. Look at Table B: 7, 12, 19, 23. None of these end in 0 or 5. That means you can't use Scale of 5 or Scale of 10. Scale of 2 is the only way to show every number exactly. Table B has to use Scale of 2.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Scale of 10. Completed example showing strategic scale choice.

beats.text: You learned to choose scales. Not just use them. You check if the biggest number fits. You check the last digits to look for multiples of 5. Then you pick what's easiest to read without too many lines.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Scale of 10. Completed example.

beats.text: That's strategic thinking. You're not just following rules. You're making decisions.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Scale of 10. Completed example.

beats.text: Next time, you'll use your graphs to solve problems and answer questions. The scale you choose will help you find answers faster.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Scale of 10. Completed example.
